text: The hernia was initially reduced by retracting the omentum out of the sac.
In this first patient, we dissected the peritoneum and falciform ligament to expose the superior margin of the defect and the abdominal wall fascia.
External pressure on the anterior abdominal wall is placed in order to facilitate getting a deep bite of the fascia.
Several interrupted horizontal mattress sutures were used.
Here we initially used a proline but did not like the potential for fraying that occurs with the use of intracorporeal knot pusher.

The endostitch can make the intracorporeal suturing somewhat technically easier, although in order to gain an adequate bite on the supra aspect of the defect, we again use pressure on the anterior abdominal wall.
This is the completed repair, and these are the incisions upon completion.
The identical approach is used with two 5 millimeter and one 10-12 millimeter port.
The contents are reduced.
And in this setting, the hernia sac is reduced as well.
We began by incising the peritoneum of the sac circumferentially around the defect.
and continuing to deliver herniated fat as well as other properitoneal fat and pericardial fat into the abdomen.
The xiphoid is being dissected and one can visualize the linea alba ascending up over the xiphoid.
The remainder of the contents of the sac and the sac itself are divided.
On the right lateral aspect, this is the pleural cavity which is being inadvertently divided along with the sac.
The pleural defect will be visualized shortly.
In the meantime, we typically lower our insufflation pressure from 15 to 10 millimeters to prevent tension physiology.
The sac is divided away from the rest of the omentum, placed in a bag, morcellated, and brought out the umbilical port site.
This is the small rent in the pleura, which was managed with a small catheter placed in the inframammary crease.
We began our repair in a similar fashion, bringing the layers of the hernia defect together.
A combination of suturing techniques were used, both traditional and the endo stitch.
One can see the persistent pneumothorax tenting the right hemidiaphragm.
We then checked the repair for persistent defects and completely closed these.
Once the repair was completed, we then connected our pleural catheter to suction to aspirate the pneumothorax and then the catheter was removed.
